content:
The probe checked /health. The JVM was still loading classes.

6 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

I probably should have known this was a thing. Readiness passed at 14 seconds. The JVM finished booting at 47 seconds. Kubernetes sent 30% of traffic to pods that returned 503 for half a minute each.
Rolling deploy. 40 pods. Readiness probe: HTTP GET /health every 5 seconds, successThreshold: 1, failureThreshold: 3.
Our /health endpoint returned 200 OK as soon as Spring Boot registered the controller. It did not wait for connection pools, cache warmers, or feature flag clients. The probe lied. Kubernetes believed it.
The hot take nobody wanted to hear
Readiness probes are not “is the process up.” They are “is this pod ready to take customer traffic.”
We had conflated liveness with readiness with “can something respond to curl.” Three different questions. One endpoint. Wrong answer for two of them.
The deploy looked green in Argo. 40/40 ready. Error rate climbed to 8.4% anyway — because “ready” meant “Tomcat listening,” not “app usable.”
I didn’t know /health could pass before Hibernate finished schema validation. I do now.
What actually happened during rollout
Deployment strategy: RollingUpdate, maxSurge: 25%, maxUnavailable: 0.
New pods spun up. Readiness passed on attempt 3 (~14 seconds). Endpoints controller added them to the Service. kube-proxy started routing.
First real customer request hit a pod still initializing:

• Database connection pool: 3/20 connections ready

• Redis client: reconnecting after DNS propagation delay

• Local cache: empty — every request cold-started

• Feature flags: default-off until LaunchDarkly SDK synced
P99 latency on new pods: 4,200ms. Error rate on new pods alone: 34% — mostly 503 from a @ControllerAdvice that rejected requests when ApplicationReadyEvent had not fired.
Old pods handled traffic fine. The mix killed us — 30% new (broken), 70% old (fine). Global error rate: 8.4%. Not enough to auto-rollback. Enough to page.
Duration: 6 minutes 18 seconds until all new pods fully warmed. Longest single pod: 52 seconds from container start to first successful checkout.
Checkout failures in window: ~2,100. Estimated cost: $8,700.
HPA made it worse briefly. New pods passed readiness, joined endpoint set, received traffic, threw 503s, CPU looked low because they rejected fast. HPA saw headroom, scaled up more broken pods. 40 became 52 before someone paused autoscaling.
Not HPA’s fault. Readiness lied. Autoscaler did arithmetic on fake capacity.
Ingress controller logs told the story we missed for 20 minutes:
upstream prematurely closed connection
502/503 mix from checkout-pod-xxxxx30% of upstream backends unhealthy from the client’s perspective while Kubernetes said 100% ready.
The probe config we had
readinessProbe:
httpGet:
path: /health
port: 8080
initialDelaySeconds: 5
periodSeconds: 5
successThreshold: 1
failureThreshold: 3
startupProbe:
# missing — we didn't know we needed itNo startup probe. Readiness started checking at 5 seconds. App needed 47.
What /health actually checked:
@GetMapping("/health")
public ResponseEntity<String> health() {
return ResponseEntity.ok("UP");
}Literal string. No dependency checks. I probably should have grep’d this before the deploy. I didn’t.
What /health/ready should have checked:

• Database: SELECT 1 with 200ms timeout

• Redis: PING

• Feature flags: SDK initialized flag

• Internal: ApplicationReadyEvent published
We added all of this after the incident. Obvious in hindsight. Expensive in foresight.
JVM boot breakdown we traced afterward with -XX:+PrintGCApplicationStoppedTime and async profiler:

• 0–8s — container start, JVM launch

• 8–14s — Spring context scan, /health endpoint registered ← readiness passed here

• 14–31s — Hibernate metadata, connection pool init

• 31–47s — Redis client, cache warm, feature flag sync, ApplicationReadyEvent
Readiness at 14s. Useful work finished at 47s. 33 seconds of lying.
Class loading alone: 8,400 classes. Fat JAR problem layered on probe problem. We later split readiness from startup AND trimmed classpath. Two fixes, one incident.
What fixed it — and what we almost did wrong
First instinct: increase initialDelaySeconds to 60. That works until the next slow node takes 61 seconds. Brittle.
Better fix — startup probe shields readiness:
startupProbe:
httpGet:
path: /health/startup
port: 8080
periodSeconds: 5
failureThreshold: 24 # 120 seconds max startupreadinessProbe:
httpGet:
path: /health/ready
port: 8080
periodSeconds: 5
failureThreshold: 3/health/startup — process is up, still initializing. /health/ready — dependencies connected, safe for traffic. /health/live — deadlock detection only, keep it dumb.
Second fix: preStop hook with sleep:
lifecycle:
preStop:
exec:
command: ["sh", "-c", "sleep 15"]Gives in-flight requests time to finish before endpoint removal. We had been dropping connections on rollout too — separate problem, same deploy.
Third fix: minReadySeconds: 10 on the Deployment — pod must stay ready for 10 seconds before counting available. Stops flapping pods from joining the pool.
I didn’t know about minReadySeconds until someone in the war room Slack thread mentioned it. Three years on Kubernetes. Still learning basic knobs.
Verification command during deploy:
kubectl get pods -l app=checkout -o json | \
jq -r '.items[] | "\(.metadata.name) ready=\(.status.conditions[]|select(.type=="Ready")|.status) restarts=\(.status.containerStatuses[0].restartCount)"'Watch new pods hit Ready, then curl through the Service with a canary header before trusting the rollout.
PodDisruptionBudget was minAvailable: 80% — fine for node drains, irrelevant here. Nobody checked whether new pods counted toward "available" before they could serve. They did. Kubernetes math was correct per spec. Spec was wrong for our app.
Rolling update parameters we tuned:
spec:
strategy:
rollingUpdate:
maxSurge: 1
maxUnavailable: 0
minReadySeconds: 15Slower rollouts. maxSurge: 1 means one new pod at a time — easier to watch, harder to blast 30% traffic at half-booted JVMs.
Argo Rollouts evaluation started next quarter — canary analysis on error rate before full promotion. Incident accelerated the roadmap by two sprints.
Synthetic check added post-deploy: external cron hits /api/checkout/health/ready through public ingress every 30 seconds during deploy windows. Catches "Kubernetes ready, customer path broken" gaps internal probes miss.
Load test in staging with readiness probe disabled vs enabled — error rate delta 0.2% vs 12% during simulated deploy. Reproduced in one hour. Should have run before prod. I probably should have suggested it. I didn’t.
Liveness vs readiness separation — we had been using the same port for both. Split them:
livenessProbe:
httpGet:
path: /health/live
port: 8080
periodSeconds: 10
failureThreshold: 3Liveness checks JVM deadlocks only — no database calls. Readiness checks dependencies. Mixing them caused earlier incidents where DB blip restarted pods instead of removing them from rotation.
Init containers considered for cache warming — rejected for complexity. Startup probe + readiness gating was enough once implemented correctly. Your mileage may vary if warm-up requires minutes of batch loading.
On-call runbook now includes: “If error rate spikes during deploy, check kubectl get endpoints count vs ready pods and curl /health/ready on newest pod IP directly." Bypasses Service abstraction. Finds liars faster.
Docker War Room — CrashLoopBackOff and healthcheck misconfiguration patterns. Kubernetes runs on containers; half our failure was container boot semantics pretending to be orchestration bugs.
30 Production Incidents That Cost $10K+ — case file #17: probe green, app not ready, rolling deploy customer impact.
The lesson I keep relearning
Green in the cluster is not green for customers.
Readiness is a contract with the load balancer. If your contract says “I’m ready” when you’re not, Kubernetes will do exactly what you asked — route traffic to a broken pod and report success while doing it.
Check what your /health endpoint actually checks. I wish I'd done that before lunch rush on a Tuesday.
Platform team added deploy annotation requirement — no production rollout without link to staging deploy video showing /health/ready response includes "dependencies":"ok". Feels bureaucratic. Saved us once already when someone tried to ship the old /health stub back.
The 503 body we returned during boot was too honest: "Application not ready". Load balancers retried aggressively, amplifying traffic on broken pods. Changed to 503 with Retry-After: 5 header and fixed retry storm contribution.
If your org runs Java on Kubernetes, grep your codebase for @GetMapping("/health") today. Not tomorrow. Before lunch rush on a Tuesday.
Three months later, same team, zero 503 deploy spikes. Same JVM. Same traffic. Different probes. The fix was never “Kubernetes is hard” — it was “our health endpoint lied.”
I keep a sticky note on my monitor now: Ready ≠ Usable. Kubernetes believes what you tell it.
Green in the cluster is not green for customers.

Rolling deploys and probe semantics show up constantly in senior interviews and on-call screens. Froquiz covers Kubernetes, Docker, and production scenario questions — 10,000+ total, with a Senior Dev Challenge that tests triage under pressure.
→ Froquiz
